perturbation.original_text: The "Confidential Information" is that confidential, proprietary, and trade secret information being disclosed by the disclosing party described as (please be specific): (a) Yahoo Confidential Information (owned by Yahoo and any of its affiliates): Resume and Employment Related. (b) Participant Confidential Information: Resume and Employment Related.
perturbation.changed_text: The "Confidential Information" is any information, whether or not it is marked or identified as such, disclosed by either party, including but not limited to information related to resumes and employment. The definition of confidential information shall be at the sole discretion of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include any information regardless of marking and granting sole discretion to the disclosing party creates ambiguity and potential overreach, making the agreement less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts CUTSA by removing the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. CUTSA requires that to be considered a trade secret, information must be subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Defining 'Confidential Information' at the sole discretion of the disclosing party undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts CUTSA, as the original contract had a more defined scope of 'Confidential Information,' while the changed contract grants the disclosing party sole discretion, potentially undermining the 'reasonable efforts' requirement to maintain secrecy. The Law Explanation specifies that CUTSA necessitates 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy for information to be considered a trade secret, and the changed text weakens this requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), referencing Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11], indicating that the contract falls under its jurisdiction. By removing the need for reasonable effort to maintain secrecy, the changed text goes against the spirit and potentially the requirements of CUTSA, and therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the other party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information and each of the parties represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the other party with a degree of care it deems appropriate, but no less than minimal care. Each party makes no representation regarding the level of care it uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the standard of care to 'no less than minimal care' and removing the representation about the care taken with one's own confidential information weakens the protection afforded to confidential information, creating uncertainty about the enforceability of this clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by CUTSA for trade secret protection. By stating 'no less than minimal care,' the contract fails to ensure the level of protection necessary to qualify as a trade secret under California law. A minimal level of care is not necessarily 'reasonable under the circumstances'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction by weakening the standard of care for protecting confidential information. The original contract requires each party to treat the other's confidential information with the same degree of care they use for their own, representing reasonable care. The changed text lowers this standard to 'no less than minimal care' and removes the representation about self-protection. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by CUTSA for trade secret protection. The provided law snippet, California Code, CIV 3426, identifies the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By replacing 'reasonable care' with 'minimal care,' the contract contradicts the implied requirement of reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially rendering the confidential information ineligible for trade secret protection under California law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the laws of the United States of America and by the laws of the State of California as such laws are applied to agreements entered into and to be performed entirely within California between California residents.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the principles of equity and fairness, as determined by the presiding officer of the court, regardless of any conflicting laws of the United States of America or the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the choice of law with 'principles of equity and fairness' introduces significant uncertainty and contradicts established legal frameworks, potentially rendering the entire agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1644 defines how the words in a contract are to be understood. The alteration allows a presiding officer to use principles of equity and fairness rather than the actual statutory definitions of terms in the contract. This opens the door for subjective interpretation and contradicts the objective standards of contract interpretation established by law, potentially altering the parties' obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1644. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces governing laws with 'principles of equity and fairness,' directly contradicting the original contract's specified legal framework. This undermines the objective standards for contract interpretation established by law. Snippet 1, from California Civil Code Section 1644, states that contract words should be understood in their ordinary sense rather than strict legal meaning unless a special meaning is provided. The change allows subjective interpretation, deviating from the legally recognized definitions and thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notices shall be sent to the addresses set forth at the end of this Agreement or such other address as either party may specify in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Notices shall be sent to the addresses last known by the sender, regardless of whether the recipient still resides there, or any address that has previously been used for communication between the parties. Electronic communication is strictly prohibited unless expressly requested, even if there is evidence of confirmed receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying 'last known address' and prohibiting electronic communication conflicts with standard legal requirements for effective notice, potentially leading to disputes over proper notification and rendering certain contractual actions invalid.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1013
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1013 which governs service by mail. By stating that notice can be sent to the 'last known address', the agreement fails to ensure actual notice or attempted service. This conflicts with the 'reasonably diligent effort' requirements. Section 1013 also allows for certain extensions when service is done through the mail. The prohibition of electronic communication directly contradicts modern practices that allow service in other manners besides mail.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts CCP 1013(a) regarding service by mail. By allowing notice to be sent to the 'last known address' regardless of current residency, it fails to ensure actual or attempted service as required by law. The prohibition of electronic communication further contradicts modern service practices beyond mail, undermining effective notification. CCP 1013(a) specifies addressing the notice to the 'office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence', a more diligent standard than 'last known address'.
